FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study, 8446 adolescents aged 9-10 at baseline 2016-2018 with 2-year follow-up

FT_IC2.evidence: Only screen types measured: television/movie watching, video game playing, video streaming. No qualifying smartphone variable (ownership, app use, bedtime phone use, time on phone with measurement detail) and no qualifying social media variable. TV-only and unspecified gaming do not qualify per B5 and B7.

FT_IC3.evidence: IC2 not met; inferential test status not evaluated

FT_IC4.evidence: IC2 not met; numeric extraction not evaluated

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD analysis using mixed-effects models

FT_IC6.evidence: No duplication; publication in 2026 Academic Pediatrics with unique DOI

FT_EC7_reason: Paper excluded at IC2 (no qualifying smartphone/SM variable); EC7 evaluation not applicable.
notes: Paper predictor is neighborhood quality (COI, SVI, ADI indices) predicting screen time. Screen type too generic (TV/gaming/streaming without platform/app detail). EXCLUDED: fails IC2.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study prospective cohort, Year 2 baseline (2018-2020) ages 11-12, N=8,119; 47.5% female; 43.8% racial/ethnic minority

FT_IC2.evidence: Three qualifying scales with separate coefficients: Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ), Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), Mobile Phone Involvement Questionnaire (MPIQ). SMAQ = qualifying social media (Bergen SM scale equivalent); MPIQ = qualifying smartphone per binding rule B1 (separate β for mobile phone involvement).

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear and logistic regression analyses with coefficients and p-values; abstract reports associations with statistical significance (p-values); Year 2 predictors vs. Year 3 outcomes.

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract reports p-values for associations; PDF at 'claude-search-subrepo/data/pdfs_include/10.1016_j.amepre.2025.108248.pdf' available for full coefficient extraction (has_pdf: true). Numeric evidence confirmed in abstract narrative of associations.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis; prospective cohort design with regression models predicting mental health and behavioral outcomes from baseline problematic screen use measures.

FT_IC6.evidence: Publication in American Journal of Preventive Medicine 2026, unique DOI 10.1016/j.amepre.2025.108248; no duplicate or preprint/abstract status indicated.

FT_EC7_reason: Smartphone/social media are INDEPENDENT variables predicting mental health and behavioral outcomes (depressive symptoms, suicidal behaviors, sleep disturbance, substance use). This is SM → non-SM-outcome analysis, not demographic-only → SM. Binding rule B18 disqualifies: paper has SM-as-IV → outcome analysis, so FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: INCLUDE: meets IC1–IC6. Prospective design with separate addiction-style scales (SMAQ, MPIQ, VGAQ). Problematic/addiction-style measures are still qualifying SM/mobile variables per protocol. SM predicts mental health outcomes.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study prospective cohort, N=7,528, age range 8-13 years (mean 10), 51.1% male, 41.8% non-White. Baseline 2016-2018, Year 2 follow-up 2018-2020.

FT_IC2.evidence: Daily social media use measured by group-based trajectory modeling (three trajectories: no/very low, low increasing, high increasing). Qualifies as 'time on SM' per binding rule B1 (social media time as separate variable with own coefficient in regression models).

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression models estimating associations between social media trajectory groups and cognitive outcomes (LMT, RAVLT); coefficients and 95% CIs reported for each comparison.

FT_IC4.evidence: Abstract reports multiple β coefficients and 95% CIs: e.g., 'B: -1.38; 95% CI: -1.82, -0.94' for RAVLT Initial Trials (low increasing vs. none); 'B: -1.90; 95% CI: -2.76, -1.04' for high increasing use. PDF at 'claude-search-subrepo/data/pdfs_include/10.1016_j.lana.2026.101454.pdf' available (has_pdf: true).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis with prospective cohort design; linear regression models with baseline and Year 2 data predicting Year 2 cognitive outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Publication in Lancet Regional Health. Americas 2026, unique DOI 10.1016/j.lana.2026.101454; no duplication indicated.

FT_EC7_reason: Social media use (measured by trajectory groups) is the INDEPENDENT variable predicting cognitive outcomes (spatial/verbal memory). This is social media → non-SM-outcome (cognition) analysis. Condition (a) of FT-EC7 requires SM to be DEPENDENT variable in EVERY analysis; here SM is always the predictor. Binding rule B18 disqualifies: paper has SM-as-IV → outcome analysis.
notes: INCLUDE: meets IC1–IC6. Prospective design with baseline-to-Year-2 follow-up. Social media trajectories (time-based groups) are qualifying SM variable per B1. Outcome is cognitive performance (non-SM). Strong numeric evidence in abstract.
